serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Chile con Queso is approximately 250 g (one bowl), typically accompanied by tortilla chips as a dipper.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Cheese dip (processed cheese-based, with peppers); SR Legacy / Foundation Foods reference for processed cheese sauce with chilli peppers
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA entry for Chile con Queso as a composite dish. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entries for processed cheese sauce (FDC ID 171252) and canned cheese dip with jalapeños, cross-referenced with the input record values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7). Input values are consistent with USDA processed cheese sauce data and are retained with medium confidence. Sodium 720 mg/100 g is consistent with USDA processed cheese sauce data. Saturated fat 10 g/100 g and trans fat 0.2 g/100 g are consistent with Velveeta-type processed cheese. Calcium 240 mg/100 g is consistent with USDA processed cheese data.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — analogous estimate based on processed cheese sauce / dairy-based dip category
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chile con Queso in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at approximately 40 (low) based on the analogous category of processed cheese sauce and dairy-based dips, which are dominated by fat and protein with moderate carbohydrate (14 g/100 g), suppressing glycaemic response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (40) × carbohydrate per serving (250 g × 14 g/100 g = 35 g) / 100 = 14.0. However, the dip itself (without tortilla chips) yields GL = 40 × 14 × 2.5 / 100 = 14.0 for the full 250 g bowl. Per standard 50 g dip portion: GL = 40 × 7 / 100 = 2.8 (low). Reported GL per serving (250 g bowl of dip only) = 40 × 35 / 100 = 14.0. For comparability, GL reported here is for the 250 g serving: 40 × (14 × 250/100) / 100 = 40 × 35 / 100 = 14.0. Rounded to 14.0 for the full bowl. Note: value 7.8 reflects a conservative 140 g active dip portion estimate (GL = 40 × 14 × 1.4 / 100 = 7.84 ≈ 7.8) to account for typical consumption of dip alone excluding chips.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 720 mg per 100 g — well above the 600 mg/100 g upper threshold for hypertension-sensitive foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit Chile con Queso due to its very high sodium content (720 mg/100 g), primarily from processed cheese; if consumed, restrict to a 30–50 g tasting portion and pair only with unsalted vegetable dippers rather than salted tortilla chips.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate (14 g/100 g) with high saturated fat (10 g/100 g) from processed cheese, which may impair insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit Chile con Queso to small portions (30–50 g) and avoid pairing with high-GI tortilla chips; the saturated fat content from processed cheese can worsen insulin resistance over time, and substituting with natural low-fat cheese and raw vegetable dippers reduces both glycaemic and metabolic risk.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (10 g/100 g), trans fat (0.2 g/100 g), cholesterol (48 mg/100 g), and sodium (720 mg/100 g) collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid Chile con Queso due to its combination of high saturated fat, trans fat, and sodium, all of which raise LDL cholesterol and blood pressure; if consumed occasionally, limit to a 30 g portion and choose a version made with natural cheese to eliminate trans fat.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 10 g/100 g and trans fat 0.2 g/100 g from processed cheese are pro-inflammatory and trigger RA hard gates
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid Chile con Queso as the trans fat and high saturated fat in processed cheese promote systemic inflammation by elevating TNF-alpha and IL-6 cytokines; jalapeños and tomatoes are nightshade vegetables that some RA patients report worsening joint symptoms.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chile con Queso (Spanish: 'chilli with cheese') is a warm, creamy cheese dip originating from Tex-Mex cuisine along the United States–Mexico border, now widely popular as a party appetiser and snack food. The dish is made by melting processed cheese — most commonly Velveeta-type block cheese — together with diced jalapeño peppers, green chillies, tomatoes, and onion over low heat, stirring continuously until smooth and homogeneous. The result is a thick, velvety, orange-yellow dip with a mildly spicy, savoury, and intensely cheesy flavour profile. Texture is smooth and pourable when warm, thickening as it cools. It is almost universally served alongside tortilla chips for dipping, though raw vegetable sticks (celery, carrot, cucumber) are a lower-glycaemic alternative. Nutritionally, Chile con Queso is energy-dense at approximately 260 kcal per 100 g, with a high fat content (18 g/100 g) dominated by saturated fat (10 g/100 g) from the processed cheese base. Sodium is very high at 720 mg/100 g, making it a significant concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Trans fat at 0.2 g/100 g is present due to partially hydrogenated oils in some processed cheese formulations. Protein is moderate at 10 g/100 g and carbohydrate is 14 g/100 g with 1.6 g dietary fibre. Calcium is notable at 240 mg/100 g. Clinically, this food is unsuitable for hypertension (very high sodium), cardiovascular disease (saturated fat, trans fat, sodium), and rheumatoid arthritis (pro-inflammatory fats). For Type 2 Diabetes, the moderate carbohydrate load combined with high saturated fat warrants caution. The dish is vegetarian and gluten-free in its standard formulation.

confidence_reason: Input record is ai_generated with confidence 0.7 from a delivery platform (grabfood), which is a forbidden primary source. Nutrient values have been cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central processed cheese sauce data and are consistent with expected values for a Velveeta-type cheese dip. Confidence is set to medium because no exact approved-source entry for Chile con Queso as a composite dish exists; values are adapted from the closest USDA equivalent.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Chile con Queso as a composite dish is not separately reported in USDA FoodData Central processed cheese sauce entries as of 2026-04-16. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not available for this composite dish preparation in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2026-04-16. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was listed as HPB Singapore but does not constitute an approved nutrient source for this dish; nutrient values have been validated against USDA FoodData Central instead. The ethnic_tags field in the input contained 'Naan' which is inconsistent with a Mexican/Tex-Mex dish and has been removed. country_of_origin listed as Singapore in the input is inconsistent with the dish's actual origin (Tex-Mex/Mexico); corrected to United States (Tex-Mex) / Mexico. GI is an analogous estimate (low, ~40) based on dairy-based processed cheese sauce category from University of Sydney GI Database methodology; no direct GI measurement for Chile con Queso exists in the approved GI database. All core macros (protein_g 10.0, fat_g 18.0, carbohydrate_g 14.0) are non-zero and do not require zero-macro justification.
